title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: table summarizes recommended measures variable type. PRE = Proportional Reduction Error. measures quantify much knowing one variable reduces prediction error . functions accept contingency table (class table, typically xtabs() table()).

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: assoc_measures() computes available measures : useful exploratory analysis. reporting, pick measure matches variable types.
code: tbl <- xtabs(~ smoking + education, data = sochealth) assoc_measures(tbl) #> Measure                            Estimate     SE  CI lower  CI upper        p  #> Cramer's V                            0.136     --     0.079     0.191  < 0.001  #> Contingency Coefficient               0.134     --        --        --  < 0.001  #> Lambda symmetric                      0.000  0.000     0.000     0.000       --  #> Lambda R|C                            0.000  0.000     0.000     0.000       --  #> Lambda C|R                            0.000  0.000     0.000     0.000       --  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau R|C             0.018  0.008     0.003     0.034    0.023  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau C|R             0.008  0.003     0.001     0.014    0.022  #> Uncertainty Coefficient symmetric     0.011  0.005     0.002     0.021    0.021  #> Uncertainty Coefficient R|C           0.018  0.008     0.003     0.032    0.021  #> Uncertainty Coefficient C|R           0.009  0.004     0.001     0.016    0.021  #> Goodman-Kruskal Gamma                -0.268  0.056    -0.378    -0.158  < 0.001  #> Kendall's Tau-b                      -0.126  0.027    -0.180    -0.073  < 0.001  #> Kendall's Tau-c                      -0.117  0.026    -0.167    -0.067  < 0.001  #> Somers' D R|C                        -0.091  0.020    -0.131    -0.052  < 0.001  #> Somers' D C|R                        -0.175  0.038    -0.249    -0.101  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Cramer’s V measures strength association two nominal variables. ranges 0 (association) 1 (perfect association). Pass detail = TRUE confidence interval p-value. p-value tests null hypothesis association using Pearson chi-squared test.
code: tbl <- xtabs(~ smoking + education, data = sochealth) cramer_v(tbl) #> [1] 0.1356677 cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.136     0.079     0.191  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: 2x2 tables, Phi equivalent Cramer’s V. Unlike V, Phi can negative table 2x2, indicating direction association. p-value tests H0: association (Pearson chi-squared test).
code: tbl_22 <- xtabs(~ smoking + physical_activity, data = sochealth) phi(tbl_22, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.006     0.000     0.063  0.839

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: contingency coefficient alternative Cramer’s V. upper bound depends table dimensions, makes harder compare across tables different sizes. p-value tests H0: association (Pearson chi-squared test).
code: contingency_coef(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.134        --        --  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: variables ordinal (ordered factors), measures account ordering appropriate Cramer’s V.

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Gamma ranges -1 +1. ignores tied pairs, makes sensitive direction association tends overestimate strength many ties. positive value means higher values one variable tend occur higher values . p-value tests H0: Gamma = 0 using Wald z-test.
code: tbl_ord <- xtabs(~ self_rated_health + education, data = sochealth) gamma_gk(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.310     0.238     0.383  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Tau-b adjusts ties ranges -1 +1. generally preferred Gamma square near-square tables. p-value tests H0: Tau-b = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: kendall_tau_b(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.205     0.155     0.254  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Tau-c similar Tau-b adjusts rectangular tables number rows columns differ. p-value tests H0: Tau-c = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: kendall_tau_c(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.200     0.151     0.248  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Somers’ D asymmetric measure: distinguishes dependent independent variable. default, row variable treated dependent (D(R|C)). p-value tests H0: D = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: somers_d(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.208     0.157     0.258  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: measures answer specific question: much knowing column variable reduce error predicting row variable (vice versa)?

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Lambda measures proportional reduction classification error. can equal zero even variables associated, modal category change across columns. p-value tests H0: Lambda = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: tbl <- xtabs(~ self_rated_health + education, data = sochealth) lambda_gk(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.012     0.000     0.039  0.389

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Tau measures proportional reduction error predicting row variable column variable, using full distribution (just mode). p-value tests H0: Tau = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: goodman_kruskal_tau(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.017     0.008     0.026  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: uncertainty coefficient (Theil’s U) based entropy. measures much knowing one variable reduces uncertainty . p-value tests H0: U = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: uncertainty_coef(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.028     0.016     0.040  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Yule’s Q defined 2x2 tables . ranges -1 +1 equivalent Gamma 2x2 tables. p-value tests H0: Q = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: tbl_22 <- xtabs(~ smoking + physical_activity, data = sochealth) yule_q(tbl_22, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.015    -0.126     0.155  0.839

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: cross_tab() can automatically select appropriate measure via assoc_measure = "auto" (default). variables ordered factors, picks Kendall’s Tau-b; otherwise uses Cramer’s V. can override automatic choice:

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: functions support confidence intervals via detail = TRUE. confidence level defaults 95% can changed conf_level: get estimate p-value (CI), pass conf_level = NULL:
code: cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE, conf_level = 0.99) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.176     0.103     0.248  < 0.001 cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE, conf_level = NULL) #> Estimate        p #>    0.176  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: detail = FALSE (default), functions return plain numeric scalar, R’s formatting rules apply. detail = TRUE, result uses custom print method defaults 3 decimal places. Pass digits change (p-value always uses 3 decimal places < 0.001): digits argument works assoc_measures(): can also store result re-display different precision without recalculating:
code: cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE, digits = 4) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>   0.1762    0.1203    0.2309  < 0.001 assoc_measures(tbl, digits = 2) #> Measure                            Estimate    SE  CI lower  CI upper        p  #> Cramer's V                             0.18    --      0.12      0.23  < 0.001  #> Contingency Coefficient                0.24    --        --        --  < 0.001  #> Lambda symmetric                       0.01  0.01      0.00      0.04    0.389  #> Lambda R|C                             0.00  0.00      0.00      0.00       --  #> Lambda C|R                             0.02  0.03      0.00      0.07    0.386  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau R|C              0.02  0.00      0.01      0.03  < 0.001  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau C|R              0.03  0.01      0.01      0.04  < 0.001  #> Uncertainty Coefficient symmetric      0.03  0.01      0.02      0.04  < 0.001  #> Uncertainty Coefficient R|C            0.03  0.01      0.01      0.04  < 0.001  #> Uncertainty Coefficient C|R            0.03  0.01      0.02      0.04  < 0.001  #> Goodman-Kruskal Gamma                  0.31  0.04      0.24      0.38  < 0.001  #> Kendall's Tau-b                        0.20  0.03      0.16      0.25  < 0.001  #> Kendall's Tau-c                        0.20  0.02      0.15      0.25  < 0.001  #> Somers' D R|C                          0.21  0.03      0.16      0.26  < 0.001  #> Somers' D C|R                          0.20  0.02      0.15      0.25  < 0.001 res <- cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE) print(res, digits = 5) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>  0.17617   0.12031   0.23092  < 0.001

title: Getting started with spicy
text: mean_n(), sum_n(), count_n() compute row-wise statistics across selected columns, automatic handling missing values.

title: Getting started with spicy
text: See ?varlist inspect variables, labels, values, missing data. See ?cross_tab full list arguments (weights, simulation, association measures). See ?table_categorical grouped one-way categorical tables. See ?table_continuous continuous summaries group comparisons. See ?table_continuous_lm model-based mean-comparison tables robust standard errors case weights. See ?assoc_measures complete list association statistics. title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: Use function matches type variables want report: practice: use table_categorical() smoking status, education, activity; use table_continuous() BMI, income, scale scores; use table_continuous_lm() outcomes reported simple weighted robust linear models; keep grouping variable want compare across.

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: functions use core arguments: argument pattern works cases: select chooses reported variables; defines grouping structure; labels cleans row labels; output decides result rendered exported. model-based continuous tables, pattern applies, must single predictor one linear model fit per outcome.
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = education,   labels = c("Smoking status", "Regular physical activity"),   output = "tinytable" ) table_continuous(   sochealth,   select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),   by = education,   labels = c(     bmi = "Body mass index",     wellbeing_score = "Well-being score",     life_sat_health = "Satisfaction with health"   ),   output = "tinytable" ) table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),   by = education,   weights = weight ) #> Continuous outcomes by Highest education level #>  #>  Variable                       │ M (Lower secondary)  M (Upper secondary)  #> ────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Body mass index                │        25.96                23.39         #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)  │        67.55                80.88         #>  Satisfaction with health (1-5) │         3.45                 4.39         #>  #>  Variable                       │ M (Tertiary)    p     R²    n    #> ────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────── #>  Body mass index                │    26.16      <.001  0.13  1188  #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)  │    66.52      <.001  0.19  1200  #>  Satisfaction with health (1-5) │     3.39      <.001  0.15  1192

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: three functions support reporting formats: Pick output based table going, analysis . underlying selection grouping pattern stays . want object fits naturally Word PowerPoint workflows can also rendered HTML documents, flextable good choice:
code: if (requireNamespace("flextable", quietly = TRUE)) {   table_continuous(     sochealth,     select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),     by = education,     output = "flextable"   ) }

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: summary-table helpers return regular gt, tinytable, flextable objects, can keep styling native package API. Use gt:: functions want keep gt workflow: Use tinytable:: functions want lightweight table-specific styling: Use flextable:: functions want keep working toward Office HTML document output. example shown code dark pkgdown theme reliable preview final flextable HTML rendering:
code: tab <- pkgdown_dark_gt(table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = education,   labels = c("Smoking status", "Regular physical activity"),   output = "gt" ))  tab |>   gt::tab_header(     title = "Health behaviors by education",     subtitle = "Categorical summary table"   ) |>   gt::tab_source_note(     gt::md("*Percentages are computed within each education group.*")   ) tab <- table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = education,   labels = c("Smoking status", "Regular physical activity"),   output = "tinytable" )  tab |>   tinytable::style_tt(     i = 2:3,     j = 2:5,     background = "red",     color = "white",     bold = TRUE   ) if (requireNamespace("flextable", quietly = TRUE)) {   tab <- table_continuous(     sochealth,     select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score),     by = education,     output = "flextable"   )    tab |>     flextable::theme_booktabs() |>     flextable::autofit() |>     flextable::fontsize(size = 10, part = "all") }

what: Keep the detailed options in the function-specific articles
title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: dedicated articles go deeper function: table_categorical() covers missing values, level filtering, association measures, one-way frequency-style tables. table_continuous() covers grouped descriptive statistics, parametric nonparametric tests, effect sizes. table_continuous_lm() covers estimated means slopes simple linear models, robust standard errors, case weights. Use vignette final reporting overview, consult function-specific articles need detailed controls.

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: grouped tables, provide data frame, one selected variables, grouping variable: default output "default", prints styled ASCII table console. Use output = "data.frame" get plain numeric data frame suitable processing.

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: Omit build frequency-style table selected variables:
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   output = "default" ) #> Categorical table #>  #>  Variable            │   n      %     #> ─────────────────────┼─────────────── #>  smoking             │                #>    No                │  926    78.8   #>    Yes               │  249    21.2   #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  physical_activity   │                #>    No                │  650    54.2   #>    Yes               │  550    45.8

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: table_categorical() supports several output formats. table summarizes options:

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: "gt" format produces table APA-style borders, column spanners, proper alignment:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = c(smoking, physical_activity, dentist_12m),     by = education,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = sex,   output = "tinytable" )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: Use output = "data.frame" wide numeric data frame (one row per modality), output = "long" long format (one row per modality x group):
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = smoking,   by = education,   output = "data.frame" ) #>   Variable Level Lower secondary n Lower secondary % Upper secondary n #> 1  smoking    No               179              69.6               415 #> 2  smoking   Yes                78              30.4               112 #>   Upper secondary % Tertiary n Tertiary % Total n Total %            p #> 1              78.7        332       84.9     926    78.8 2.012877e-05 #> 2              21.3         59       15.1     249    21.2 2.012877e-05 #>   Cramer's V #> 1  0.1356677 #> 2  0.1356677

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: default, table_categorical() uses variable names row headers. Use labels argument provide human-readable labels. Two forms accepted (matching table_continuous() table_continuous_lm()): named character vector keyed column name data – recommended form. listed columns relabelled; others fall back column name. positional character vector length select – legacy spicy < 0.11.0 form, kept backward compatibility.
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = c(smoking, physical_activity),     by = education,     labels = c(       smoking           = "Smoking status",       physical_activity = "Regular physical activity"     ),     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: default, table_categorical() reports Cramer’s V nominal variables automatically switches Kendall’s Tau-b variables ordered factors. Override assoc_measure: Add confidence intervals assoc_ci = TRUE. rendered formats (gt, tinytable, flextable), CI shown inline: data formats ("data.frame", "long", "excel", "clipboard"), separate CI lower CI upper columns added:
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = smoking,   by = education,   assoc_measure = "lambda",   output = "tinytable" ) pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = c(smoking, physical_activity),     by = education,     assoc_ci = TRUE,     output = "gt"   ) ) table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = smoking,   by = education,   assoc_ci = TRUE,   output = "data.frame" ) #>   Variable Level Lower secondary n Lower secondary % Upper secondary n #> 1  smoking    No               179              69.6               415 #> 2  smoking   Yes                78              30.4               112 #>   Upper secondary % Tertiary n Tertiary % Total n Total %            p #> 1              78.7        332       84.9     926    78.8 2.012877e-05 #> 2              21.3         59       15.1     249    21.2 2.012877e-05 #>   Cramer's V   CI lower  CI upper #> 1  0.1356677 0.07909264 0.1913716 #> 2  0.1356677 0.07909264 0.1913716

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: Pass survey weights weights argument. Use rescale = TRUE total weighted N matches unweighted N:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = c(smoking, physical_activity),     by = education,     weights = "weight",     rescale = TRUE,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: default, rows missing values dropped (drop_na = TRUE). Set drop_na = FALSE display “(Missing)” category:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = income_group,     by = education,     drop_na = FALSE,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: Use levels_keep display specific modalities. order specify controls display order, useful placing “(Missing)” first highlight missingness:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = income_group,     by = education,     drop_na = FALSE,     levels_keep = c("(Missing)", "Low", "High"),     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: Control number digits percentages, p-values, association measure: p_digits drives displayed precision p column small-p threshold (p_digits = 3 -> <.001, p_digits = 4 -> <.0001), matching table_continuous() table_continuous_lm().
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = smoking,     by = education,     percent_digits = 2,     p_digits = 4,     v_digits = 3,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: default (align = "decimal") numeric columns aligned decimal mark, standard scientific-publication convention used SPSS, SAS, LaTeX siunitx, native primitives gt::cols_align_decimal() tinytable::style_tt(align = "d"). Engines without native primitive (flextable, word, clipboard, ASCII print) get alignment via leading / trailing space padding, flextable / word switching body font Consolas character widths match. Pass align = "auto" revert legacy uniform right-alignment used spicy < 0.11.0: "center" "right" apply literal alignment.

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: table_categorical() returns object can coerced plain data.frame / tbl_df (stripping spicy formatting attributes) piped broom::tidy() / broom::glance() use gtsummary, modelsummary, parameters, tidyverse-stats workflow:
code: out <- table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = sex ) #> Categorical table by sex #>  #>  Variable          │ Female n  Female %  Male n  Male %  Total n  Total %   p    #> ───────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  smoking           │                                                       .713  #>    No              │   475       78.4     451     79.3     926     78.8          #>    Yes             │   131       21.6     118     20.7     249     21.2          #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  physical_activity │                                                       .832  #>    No              │   334       53.9     316     54.5     650     54.2          #>    Yes             │   286       46.1     264     45.5     550     45.8          #>  #>  Variable          │ Phi  #> ───────────────────┼───── #>  smoking           │ .01  #>    No              │      #>    Yes             │      #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌ #>  physical_activity │ .01  #>    No              │      #>    Yes             │  # One row per (variable x level x group) with broom-style columns # (outcome, level, group, n, proportion). The synthetic Total # margin is excluded so each observation is counted once. broom::tidy(out) #> # A tibble: 8 × 5 #>   outcome           level group      n proportion #>   <chr>             <chr> <chr>  <int>      <dbl> #> 1 smoking           No    Female   475      0.784 #> 2 smoking           No    Male     451      0.793 #> 3 smoking           Yes   Female   131      0.216 #> 4 smoking           Yes   Male     118      0.207 #> 5 physical_activity No    Female   334      0.539 #> 6 physical_activity No    Male     316      0.545 #> 7 physical_activity Yes   Female   286      0.461 #> 8 physical_activity Yes   Male     264      0.455  # One row per outcome with the omnibus chi-squared test and the # chosen association measure (test_type, statistic, df, p.value, # assoc_type, assoc_value, assoc_ci_lower / assoc_ci_upper, n_total). broom::glance(out) #> # A tibble: 2 × 10 #>   outcome           test_type   statistic    df p.value assoc_type assoc_value #>   <chr>             <chr>           <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>            <dbl> #> 1 physical_activity chi_squared    0.0452     1   0.832 Phi            0.00614 #> 2 smoking           chi_squared    0.136      1   0.713 Phi            0.0107  #> # ℹ 3 more variables: assoc_ci_lower <dbl>, assoc_ci_upper <dbl>, n_total <int>

what: Exporting to Excel, Word, or clipboard
title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: Excel export, provide file path: Word, use output = "word": can also copy directly clipboard pasting spreadsheet text editor:
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity, dentist_12m),   by = education,   output = "excel",   excel_path = "my_table.xlsx" ) table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity, dentist_12m),   by = education,   output = "word",   word_path = "my_table.docx" ) table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = education,   output = "clipboard" )

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: Use select one continuous outcomes single predictor: categorical predictors, table reports estimated means level. predictor dichotomous, can also show single mean difference confidence interval.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi, life_sat_health),   by = sex ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                       │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)  │   67.16      71.05          3.89         #>  Body mass index                │   25.69      26.20          0.51         #>  Satisfaction with health (1-5) │    3.51       3.59          0.08         #>  #>  Variable                       │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    n    #> ────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)  │    2.13      5.64     <.001  0.02  1200  #>  Body mass index                │    0.09      0.93      .018  0.00  1188  #>  Satisfaction with health (1-5) │   -0.06      0.22      .267  0.00  1192

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: Use vcov = "HC*" want heteroskedasticity-consistent standard errors tests: HC* family computed via [sandwich::vcovHC()] includes "HC0", "HC1", "HC2", "HC3" (default small / moderate samples), "HC4", "HC4m", "HC5".
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   vcov = "HC3",   statistic = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67.16      71.05          3.89         #>  Body mass index               │   25.69      26.20          0.51         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL   t      p     R²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.12       5.65     4.32  <.001  0.02  1200  #>  Body mass index               │   0.09       0.93     2.38   .018  0.00  1188

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: observations independent (repeated measurements per individual, students nested classes, panel data), classical HC* standard errors biased downward. Use CR* family together cluster = id_var get cluster-robust inference, dispatched clubSandwich: "CR2" recommended default (Bell & McCaffrey 2002; Pustejovsky & Tipton 2018). produces fractional Satterthwaite degrees freedom, rendered displayed test header e.g. t(8.7) F(2, 12.4). "CR1" matches Stata’s vce(cluster id) default.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sleep,   select = extra,   by = group,   vcov = "CR2",   cluster = ID,   statistic = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by group #>  #>  Variable │ M (1)  M (2)  Δ (2 - 1)  95% CI LL  95% CI UL  t(9)   p     R²   n   #> ──────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  extra    │ 0.75   2.33     1.58       0.70       2.46     4.06  .003  0.16  20

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: situations residual distribution non-standard sample small, vcov = "bootstrap" vcov = "jackknife" provide resampling-based variance estimators pure base R (dependency added): cluster supplied, bootstrap switches cluster bootstrap (Cameron, Gelbach & Miller 2008) jackknife leave-one-cluster-(Quenouille 1956). estimators use asymptotic inference: z single-coefficient contrasts chi^2(q) global Wald test k > 2 categorical predictors, rendered displayed test header.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = wellbeing_score,   by = sex,   vcov = "bootstrap",   boot_n = 1000  # default )

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: Use weights want weighted estimated means slopes model-based table: often natural summary-table function reporting workflow already relies weighted linear models.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = education,   weights = weight,   show_weighted_n = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Highest education level #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Lower secondary)  M (Upper secondary)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │        67.55                80.88         #>  Body mass index               │        25.96                23.39         #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Tertiary)    p     R²    n    Weighted n  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │    66.52      <.001  0.19  1200   1196.47    #>  Body mass index               │    26.16      <.001  0.13  1188   1183.32

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: numeric, table_continuous_lm() reports slope rather group means: need underlying returned data processing, use output = "data.frame" wide raw table output = "long" analytic long table.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = age,   vcov = "HC3" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Age (years) #>  #>  Variable                      │  B    95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 0.04    -0.02      0.10      .176  0.00  1200  #>  Body mass index               │ 0.04     0.02      0.05     <.001  0.02  1188

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: table_continuous_lm() supports four effect sizes via effect_size argument. derived fitted model displayed coefficients R², table stays internally consistent — invariant vcov (choice classical HC* standard errors changes contrast SE, CI, test statistic standardized effect size ). Cohen’s d Hedges’ g conventions two-group comparisons (Cohen 1988; Hedges Olkin 1985; APA 2020) require exactly two non-empty levels: Hedges’ g applies small-sample correction J = 1 - 3/(4·df_resid - 1). generally preferred raw d published reports (Goulet-Pelletier Cousineau 2018): categorical predictors three levels (numeric predictors), use Hays’ omega² less biased estimate population variance explained (Hays 1963; Olejnik Algina 2003; Lakens 2013): Cohen’s f² (= R² / (1 - R²)) effect size familiar power-analysis frameworks (e.g. G*Power) defined predictor type:
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = smoking,   effect_size = "d" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Current smoker #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (No)  M (Yes)  Δ (Yes - No)  95% CI LL  #> ───────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 69.36    67.65      -1.72        -3.91    #>  Body mass index               │ 25.96    25.93      -0.03        -0.55    #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI UL   p     R²     d     n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   0.47     .124  0.00  -0.11  1175  #>  Body mass index               │   0.49     .905  0.00  -0.01  1163 table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = smoking,   effect_size = "g" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Current smoker #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (No)  M (Yes)  Δ (Yes - title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: Setting effect_size_ci = TRUE adds confidence interval effect size. implementation uses modern noncentral-distribution inversion approach — consensus standard commercial statistical software (Stata esize / estat esize, SAS PROC TTEST PROC GLM EFFECTSIZE) mainstream R packages (effectsize, MOTE, TOSTER, effsize): Noncentral t inversion "d" "g" (Steiger Fouladi 1997; Goulet-Pelletier Cousineau 2018; Cousineau Goulet-Pelletier 2021), nominal coverage across sample sizes — unlike older Hedges-Olkin normal approximation biased small samples. Noncentral F inversion "omega2" "f2" (Steiger 2004; Smithson 2003). printed rendered outputs, effect size displayed bracketed CI (article-ready): CI level follows ci_level (default 0.95). programmatic access, wide raw output exposes separate numeric columns, long output always includes : weights supplied, four effect sizes (CIs) computed weighted least-squares fit, keeping consistent weighted contrast CI (DuMouchel Duncan 1983). right convention case-weighted reporting; propensity-score balance assessment (Austin Stuart 2015) complex-survey designs, dedicated packages (cobalt::bal.tab() survey) appropriate.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = smoking,   effect_size = "g",   effect_size_ci = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Current smoker #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (No)  M (Yes)  Δ (Yes - No)  95% CI LL  #> ───────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 69.36    67.65      -1.72        -3.91    #>  Body mass index               │ 25.96    25.93      -0.03        -0.55    #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI UL   p     R²            g           #> ───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   0.47     .124  0.00  -0.11 [-0.25, 0.03]  #>  Body mass index               │   0.49     .905  0.00  -0.01 [-0.15, 0.13]  #>  #>  Variable                      │  n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 1175  #>  Body mass index               │ 1163 table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = wellbeing_score,   by = smoking,   effect_size = "g",   effect_size_ci = TRUE,   output = "data.frame" ) #>                                      Variable   M (No)  M (Yes) Δ (Yes - No) #> wellbeing_score WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) 69.36317 67.64538    -1.717793 #>                 95% CI LL 95% CI UL         p          R²          g #> wellbeing_score -3.906437 0.4708504 0.1238546 0.002017481 -0.1098571 #>                 effect_size_ci_lower effect_size_ci_upper    n #> wellbeing_score           -0.2497291           0.03006164 1175 out <- table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = wellbeing_score,   by = smoking,   effect_size = "g",   effect_size_ci = TRUE,   output = "long" ) out[, c("variable", "es_type", "es_value", "es_ci_lower", "es_ci_upper")] #>          variable es_type   es_value es_ci_lower es_ci_upper #> 1 wellbeing_score       g -0.1098571  -0.2497291  0.03006164 #> 2 wellbeing_score    <NA>         NA          NA          NA

text: Add summarize variables across categories. supplied, Welch-test p-value column added automatically: main pattern reporting continuous variables across groups education, sex, treatment arm, survey wave. what: Add test statistics and effect sizes
title: Continuous summary tables in R
text: Grouped tables can also report test statistic effect size alongside default p-value column: Use test = "student" equal-variance parametric tests test = "nonparametric" rank-based comparisons: effect_size = TRUE auto-selects canonical measure chosen test number groups: Hedges’ g (parametric, 2 groups), eta-squared (parametric, 3+ groups), rank-biserial r (nonparametric, 2 groups), epsilon-squared (nonparametric, 3+ groups). pick specific measure explicitly, pass character name: Allowed values "none" (default), "auto" (= legacy TRUE), "hedges_g", "eta_sq", "r_rb", "epsilon_sq". Incompatible explicit choices (e.g. "eta_sq" two groups, "hedges_g" test = "nonparametric") trigger actionable error. need underlying columns processing, use output = "data.frame":

what: Recover labels from imported column names
title: Explore variables and build codebooks in R
text: imported files store variable name variable label column header. label_from_names() splits names form name<sep>label, renames columns, stores label proper variable label. especially useful LimeSurvey CSV exports using Export results -> Export format: CSV -> Headings: Question code & question text, column names look like "code. question text". case default separator ". ".

Current smoker" = c("No", "Yes", "No") )  out <- label_from_names(df) labelled::var_label(out) #> $age #> [1] "Age of respondent" #>  #> $edu #> [1] "Highest education level" #>  #> $smoke #> [1] "Current smoker"

title: Explore variables and build codebooks in R

title: spicy: frequency tables, cross-tabulations, and summary tables in R
text: spicy helps explore categorical, continuous, labelled survey data R. provides readable, console-first outputs survey research, descriptive statistics, reporting workflows, including frequency tables, cross-tabulations chi-squared tests effect sizes, categorical continuous summary tables, variable inspection, codebooks. spicy, can: Inspect variables varlist() vl() names, labels, values, classes, missing data. Create frequency tables R freq(). Create cross-tabulations R cross_tab(), including percentages, chi-squared tests, effect sizes. Measure associations cramer_v(), phi(), gamma_gk(), kendall_tau_b(), somers_d(), related functions. Build categorical summary tables R table_categorical() gt, tinytable, flextable, Excel, Word, clipboard export. Build continuous summary tables R table_continuous() console, gt, tinytable, flextable, Excel, Word, clipboard output. Build model-based continuous summary tables R table_continuous_lm() linear regression reporting, robust standard errors, weighted mean comparisons, console, gt, tinytable, flextable, Excel, Word, clipboard output. Generate interactive exportable codebooks code_book() labelled survey-style datasets. Extract variable labels label_from_names(), including LimeSurvey-style headers. Works labelled, factor, ordered, Date, POSIXct, common variable types. full introduction, see Getting started spicy.

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: function inspired datawizard::row_count(), provides additional flexibility: Element-wise type-safe matching using identical() allow_coercion = FALSE. ensures value type match exactly, enabling precise comparisons mixed-type columns. Support multiple values count, allowing queries like count = c(2, 3) count = c("yes", "") count several values per row. Detection special values NA, NaN, Inf, -Inf special argument — feature available row_count(). Tidyverse-native behavior: can used inside mutate() without explicitly passing data argument.

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: R automatically coerces mixed-type vectors passed count common type. example, count = c(2, "2") becomes c("2", "2"), R converts numeric character values unified type. means mixed-type checks possible runtime count passed function. ensure accurate type-sensitive matching, users avoid mixing types count explicitly.

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: strict matching enabled, value count must match type target column exactly. factor columns, means count must also factor. Supplying count = "b" (character string) match factor value, even label appears identical. common intuitive approach use count = factor("b"), works many cases. However, identical() — used internally strict comparisons — also checks internal structure factor, including order content levels. result, comparisons may still fail levels differ, even label . ensure perfect match (label levels), can reuse value taken directly data (e.g., df$x[2]). guarantees class factor levels align. However, approach works reliably selected columns factor structure.

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: ignore_case = TRUE, values involved comparison converted lowercase using tolower() matching. behavior applies character factor columns. Factors first converted character internally. Importantly, case-insensitive mode takes precedence strict type comparison: values longer compared using identical(), rather using lowercase string equality. enables flexible matching — example, "b" "B" match even allow_coercion = FALSE.

what: Example: strict vs. case-insensitive matching with factors
title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: Like datawizard::row_count(), function also supports regex-based column selection, case-insensitive string comparison, column exclusion.
code: df <- tibble::tibble(   x = factor(c("a", "b", "c")),   y = factor(c("b", "B", "a")) )  # Strict match fails with character input count_n(df, count = "b", allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 0 0  # Match works only where factor levels match exactly count_n(df, count = factor("b", levels = levels(df$x)), allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 1 0  # Case-insensitive match succeeds for both "b" and "B" count_n(df, count = "b", ignore_case = TRUE) #> [1] 1 2 0

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
code: library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: ‘dplyr’ #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:stats’: #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:base’: #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union library(tibble) library(haven)  # Basic usage df <- tibble(   x = c(1, 2, 2, 3, NA),   y = c(2, 2, NA, 3, 2),   z = c("2", "2", "2", "3", "2") ) count_n(df, count = 2) #> [1] 2 3 2 0 2 count_n(df, count = 2, allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 1 2 1 0 1 df |> mutate(num_twos = count_n(count = 2)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>       x     y z     num_twos #>   <dbl> <dbl> <chr>    <dbl> #> 1     1     2 2            2 #> 2     2     2 2            3 #> 3     2    NA 2            2 #> 4     3     3 3            0 #> 5    NA     2 2            2  # Mixed types and special values df <- tibble(   num   = c(1, 2, NA, -Inf, NaN),   char  = c("a", "B", "b", "a", NA),   fact  = factor(c("a", "b", "b", "a", "c")),   date  = as.Date(c("2023-01-01", "2023-01-01", NA, "2023-01-02", "2023-01-01")),   lab   = labelled(c(1, 2, 1, 2, NA), labels = c(No = 1, Yes = 2)),   logic = c(TRUE, FALSE, NA, TRUE, FALSE) ) count_n(df, count = 2) #> [1] 0 2 0 1 0 count_n(df, count = "b", ignore_case = TRUE) #> [1] 0 2 3 0 0 count_n(df, count = "a", select = fact) #> [1] 1 0 0 1 0 count_n(df, count = as.Date("2023-01-01"), select = date) #> [1] 1 1 0 0 1  # Count special values count_n(df, special = "NA") #> [1] 0 0 3 0 3  # Column selection strategies df <- tibble(   score_math    = c(1, 2, 2, 3, NA),   score_science = c(2, 2, NA, 3, 2),   score_lang    = c("2", "2", "2", "3", "2"),   name          = c("Jean", "Marie", "Ali", "Zoe", "Nina") ) count_n(df, select = c(score_math, score_science), count = 2) #> [1] 1 2 1 0 1 count_n(df, select = starts_with("score_"), exclude = "score_lang", count = 2) #> [1] 1 2 1 0 1 count_n(df, select = "^score_", regex = TRUE, count = 2) #> [1] 2 3 2 0 2 df |> mutate(nb_two = count_n(count = 2)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   score_math score_science score_lang name  nb_two #>        <dbl>         <dbl> <chr>      <chr>  <dbl> #> 1          1             2 2          Jean       2 #> 2          2             2 2          Marie      3 #> 3          2            NA 2          Ali        2 #> 4          3             3 3          Zoe        0 #> 5         NA             2 2          Nina       2  # Strict type-safe matching with factor columns df <- tibble(   x = factor(c("a", "b", "c")),   y = factor(c("b", "B", "a")) )  # Coercion: character "b" matches both x and y count_n(df, count = "b") #> [1] 1 1 0  # Strict match: fails because "b" is character, not factor (returns only 0s) count_n(df, count = "b", allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 0 0  # Strict match with factor value: works only where levels match count_n(df, count = factor("b", levels = levels(df$x)), allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 1 0

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: cramer_v() computes Cramer's V two-way contingency table, measuring strength association two categorical variables.

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: detail = FALSE: single numeric value (estimate). detail = TRUE conf_level non-NULL: c(estimate, ci_lower, ci_upper, p_value). detail = TRUE conf_level = NULL: c(estimate, p_value). p-value tests null hypothesis association (Pearson chi-squared test).

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: Cramer's V computed \(V = \sqrt{\chi^2 / (n \cdot (k - 1))}\), \(\chi^2\) Pearson chi-squared statistic, \(n\) total count, \(k = \min(r, c)\). point estimate matches DescTools (Signorell et al., 2024) SPSS implementations. confidence interval uses Fisher z-transformation \(V\) (\(\tanh(\mathrm{atanh}(V) \pm z_{\alpha/2} / \sqrt{n - 3})\)), differs noncentral chi-squared bootstrap CIs reported DescTools::CramerV().

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: Agresti, . (2002). Categorical Data Analysis (2nd ed.). Wiley. Liebetrau, . M. (1983). Measures Association. Sage. Signorell, . et al. (2024). DescTools: Tools Descriptive Statistics. R package.

code: cross_tab(   data,   x,   y = NULL,   by = NULL,   weights = NULL,   rescale = FALSE,   percent = c("none", "column", "row"),   include_stats = TRUE,   assoc_measure = c("auto", "cramer_v", "phi", "gamma", "tau_b", "tau_c", "somers_d",     "lambda", "none"),   assoc_ci = FALSE,   correct = FALSE,   simulate_p = FALSE,   simulate_B = 2000,   digits = NULL,   styled = TRUE,   show_n = TRUE,   decimal_mark = ".",   p_digits = 3L )

title: Cross-tabulation — cross_tab
text: data data frame. Alternatively, vector using vector-based interface. x Row variable (unquoted). y Column variable (unquoted). Mandatory; one-way tables, use freq(). Optional grouping variable expression. Can single variable combination multiple variables (e.g. interaction(vs, )). weights Optional numeric weights. rescale Logical. FALSE (default), weights used -. TRUE, rescales weights total weighted N matches raw N. percent One "none" (default), "row", "column". Unique abbreviations accepted (e.g. "n", "r", "c"). include_stats Logical. TRUE (default), computes Chi-squared association measure (see assoc_measure). assoc_measure Character. association measure report. "auto" (default) selects Kendall's Tau-b variables ordered factors Cramer's V otherwise. choices: "cramer_v", "phi", "gamma", "tau_b", "tau_c", "somers_d", "lambda", "none". assoc_ci Logical. TRUE, includes 95 percent confidence interval association measure note. Defaults FALSE. correct Logical. FALSE (default), continuity correction applied. TRUE, applies Yates correction (2x2 tables). simulate_p Logical. FALSE (default), uses asymptotic p-values. TRUE, uses Monte Carlo simulation. simulate_B Integer. title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
text: gamma_gk() computes Goodman-Kruskal Gamma statistic two-way contingency table ordinal variables.

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
code: gamma_gk(   x,   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   digits = 3L,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: gamma = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
text: Gamma computed \(\gamma = (C - D) / (C + D)\), \(C\) \(D\) numbers concordant discordant pairs. ignores tied pairs, making appropriate ordinal variables many ties. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
code: tab <- table(sochealth$education, sochealth$self_rated_health) gamma_gk(tab) #> [1] 0.3104791 gamma_gk(tab, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.310     0.238     0.383  < 0.001

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
text: object class df – base data.frame df base data.frame, tbl_df df tibble. output column names equal trimmed names (sep) , every column whose original name contained sep, "label" attribute equal label (sep). Columns whose name contain sep passed unchanged label attached.

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
text: especially useful LimeSurvey CSV exports using Export results -> Export format: CSV -> Headings: Question code & question text, column names look like "code. question text". default separator ". " match export. LimeSurvey question codes (part sep) restricted alphanumeric characters, must start letter, contain spaces special characters. column name therefore needs encode code question text, separated literal string – way recover label code alone. export uses Headings: Question code (codes ), re-export Headings: Question code & question text (inserts default ". " separator) calling function.

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
text: function raises actionable error – rather letting downstream constructor raise cryptic one – split produces: duplicate column names (two original names share prefix sep); empty column name (original name starts sep nothing ).

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
code: # LimeSurvey-style column names (default sep = ". "). df <- data.frame(   "age. Age of respondent" = c(25, 30),   "score. Total score. Manually computed." = c(12, 14),   check.names = FALSE ) out <- label_from_names(df) attr(out$age, "label") #> [1] "Age of respondent" attr(out$score, "label") #> [1] "Total score. Manually computed."  # Custom separator. df2 <- data.frame(   "id|Identifier" = 1:3,   "score|Total score" = c(10, 20, 30),   check.names = FALSE ) out2 <- label_from_names(df2, sep = "|")

title: Row Means with Optional Minimum Valid Values — mean_n
code: library(dplyr)  # Create a simple numeric data frame df <- tibble(   var1 = c(10, NA, 30, 40, 50),   var2 = c(5, NA, 15, NA, 25),   var3 = c(NA, 30, 20, 50, 10) )  # Compute row-wise mean (all values must be valid by default) mean_n(df) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Require at least 2 valid (non-NA) values per row mean_n(df, min_valid = 2) #> [1]  7.50000       NA 21.66667 45.00000 28.33333  # Require at least 50% valid (non-NA) values per row mean_n(df, min_valid = 0.5) #> [1]  7.50000       NA 21.66667 45.00000 28.33333  # Round the result to 1 decimal mean_n(df, digits = 1) #> [1]   NA   NA 21.7   NA 28.3  # Select specific columns mean_n(df, select = c(var1, var2)) #> [1]  7.5   NA 22.5   NA 37.5  # Select specific columns using a pipe df |>   select(var1, var2) |>   mean_n() #> [1]  7.5   NA 22.5   NA 37.5  # Exclude a column mean_n(df, exclude = "var3") #> [1]  7.5   NA 22.5   NA 37.5  # Select columns ending with "1" mean_n(df, select = ends_with("1")) #> [1] 10 NA 30 40 50  # Use with native pipe df |> mean_n(select = starts_with("var")) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Use inside dplyr::mutate() df |> mutate(mean_score = mean_n(min_valid = 2)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>    var1  var2  var3 mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA        7.5 #> 2    NA    NA    30       NA   #> 3    30    15    20       21.7 #> 4    40    NA    50       45   #> 5    50    25    10       28.3  # Select columns directly inside mutate() df |> mutate(mean_score = mean_n(select = c(var1, var2), min_valid = 1)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>    var1  var2  var3 mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA        7.5 #> 2    NA    NA    30       NA   #> 3    30    15    20       22.5 #> 4    40    NA    50       40   #> 5    50    25    10       37.5  # Select columns before mutate df |>   select(var1, var2) |>   mutate(mean_score = mean_n(min_valid = 1)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>    var1  var2 mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5        7.5 #> 2    NA    NA       NA   #> 3    30    15       22.5 #> 4    40    NA       40   #> 5    50    25       37.5  # Show verbose processing info df |> mutate(mean_score = mean_n(min_valid = 2, digits = 1, verbose = TRUE)) #> mean_n(): Row means computed with min_valid = 2, regex = FALSE #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>    var1  var2  var3 mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA        7.5 #> 2    NA    NA    30       NA   #> 3    30    15    20       21.7 #> 4    40    NA    50       45   #> 5    50    25    10       28.3  # Add character and grouping columns df_mixed <- mutate(df,   name = letters[1:5],   group = c("A", "A", "B", "B", "A") ) df_mixed #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>    var1  var2  var3 name  group #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr> #> 1    10     5    NA a     A     #> 2    NA    NA    30 b     A     #> 3    30    15    20 c     B     #> 4    40    NA    50 d     B     #> 5    50    25    10 e     A      # Non-numeric columns are ignored mean_n(df_mixed) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Use within mutate() on mixed data df_mixed |> mutate(mean_score = mean_n(select = starts_with("var"))) #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>    var1  var2  var3 name  group mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA a     A           NA   #> 2    NA    NA    30 b     A           NA   #> 3    30    15    20 c     B           21.7 #> 4    40    NA    50 d     B           NA   #> 5    50    25    10 e     A           28.3  # Use everything() but exclude non-numeric columns manually mean_n(df_mixed, select = everything(), exclude = "group") #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Select columns using regex mean_n(df_mixed, select = "^var", regex = TRUE) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333 mean_n(df_mixed, select = "ar", regex = TRUE) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Apply to a subset of rows (first 3) df_mixed[1:3, ] |> mean_n(select = starts_with("var")) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667  # Store the result in a new column df_mixed$mean_score <- mean_n(df_mixed, select = starts_with("var")) df_mixed #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>    var1  var2  var3 name  group mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA a     A           NA   #> 2    NA    NA    30 b     A           NA   #> 3    30    15    20 c     B           21.7 #> 4    40    NA    50 d     B           NA   #> 5    50    25    10 e     A           28.3  # With a numeric matrix mat <- matrix(c(1, 2, NA, 4, 5, NA, 7, 8, 9), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE) mat #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,]    1    2   NA #> [2,]    4    5   NA #> [3,]    7    8    9 mat |> mean_n(min_valid = 2) #> [1] 1.5 4.5 8.0

title: Phi coefficient — phi

title: Somers' D — somers_d

title: Somers' D — somers_d
text: x contingency table (class table). direction Direction prediction: "row" (default, column predicts row), "column" (row predicts column), "symmetric" (average directions). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Somers' D — somers_d
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: D = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Somers' D — somers_d
text: Somers' D asymmetric ordinal measure defined \(d = (C - D) / (C + D + T)\), \(T\) number pairs tied independent variable. symmetric version harmonic mean two asymmetric values. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Somers' D — somers_d

what: spicy: Descriptive Statistics, Summary Tables, and Data Management Tools — spicy-package
title: spicy: Descriptive Statistics, Summary Tables, and Data Management Tools — spicy-package
text: Provides tools descriptive data analysis, variable inspection, data management, tabulation workflows 'R'. Summarizes variable metadata, labels, classes, missing values, representative values, support readable frequency tables, cross-tabulations, association measures contingency tables (Cramer's V, Phi, Goodman-Kruskal Gamma, Kendall's Tau-b, Somers' D, others), categorical continuous summary tables, model-based bivariate tables continuous outcomes, including APA-style reporting outputs. Includes helpers interactive codebooks, variable label extraction, clipboard export, row-wise descriptive summaries. Designed make descriptive analysis reporting faster, clearer, easier work practice.

text: x spicy_table data.frame printed. title Optional title displayed table. Defaults "title" attribute x present. note Optional note displayed table. Defaults "note" attribute x present. padding Non-negative integer giving number extra characters added column's auto-computed width (max cell-content width header width). Defaults 2L. See build_ascii_table() precise formula migration note pre-0.11.0 string enum. first_column_line Logical. TRUE (default), adds vertical separator first column. row_total_line, column_total_line, bottom_line Logical flags controlling presence horizontal lines total rows/columns bottom table. row_total_line column_total_line default TRUE; bottom_line defaults FALSE. lines_color Character. Color table separators. Defaults "darkgrey". applied output supports ANSI colors (see crayon::has_color()). align_left_cols Integer vector column indices left-align. NULL (default), alignment auto-detected based x: freq tables -> c(1, 2) cross tables -> 1 align_center_cols Integer vector column indices center-align. Defaults integer(0). group_sep_rows Integer vector row indices light dashed separator line drawn. Defaults integer(0). total_row_idx Optional integer vector 1-based row indices identifying totals rows; defaults "total_row_idx" attribute x (set cross_tab()). See build_ascii_table(). ... Require at least 2 valid (non-NA) values per row sum_n(df, min_valid = 2) #> [1] 15 NA 65 90 85  # Require at least 50% valid (non-NA) values per row sum_n(df, min_valid = 0.5) #> [1] 15 NA 65 90 85  # Round the results to 1 decimal sum_n(df, digits = 1) #> [1] NA NA 65 NA 85  # Select specific columns sum_n(df, select = c(var1, var2)) #> [1] 15 NA 45 NA 75  # Select specific columns using a pipe df |>   select(var1, var2) |>   sum_n() #> [1] 15 NA 45 NA 75  # Exclude a column sum_n(df, exclude = "var3") #> [1] 15 NA 45 NA 75  # Select columns ending with "1" sum_n(df, select = ends_with("1")) #> [1] 10 NA 30 40 50  # Use with native pipe df |> sum_n(select = starts_with("var")) #> [1] NA NA 65 NA 85  # Use inside dplyr::mutate() df |> mutate(sum_score = sum_n(min_valid = 2)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>    var1  var2  var3 sum_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA        15 #> 2    NA    NA    30        NA #> 3    30    15    20        65 #> 4    40    NA    50        90 #> 5    50    25    10        85  # Select columns directly inside mutate() df |> mutate(sum_score = sum_n(select = c(var1, var2), min_valid = 1)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>    var1  var2  var3 sum_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA        15 #> 2    NA    NA    30        NA #> 3    30    15    20        45 #> 4    40    NA    50        40 #> 5    50    25    10        75  # Select columns before mutate df |>   select(var1, var2) |>   mutate(sum_score = sum_n(min_valid = 1)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>    var1  var2 sum_score #>   <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1    10     5        15 #> 2    NA    NA        NA #> 3    30    15        45 #> 4    40    NA        40 #> 5    50    25        75  # Show verbose message df |> mutate(sum_score = sum_n(min_valid = 2, digits = 1, verbose = TRUE)) #> sum_n(): Row sums computed with min_valid = 2, regex = FALSE #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>    var1  var2  var3 sum_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA        15 #> 2    NA    NA    30        NA #> 3    30    15    20        65 #> 4    40    NA    50        90 #> 5    50    25    10        85  # Add character and grouping columns df_mixed <- mutate(df,   name = letters[1:5],   group = c("A", "A", "B", "B", "A") ) df_mixed #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>    var1  var2  var3 name  group #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr> #> 1    10     5    NA a     A     #> 2    NA    NA    30 b     A     #> 3    30    15    20 c     B     #> 4    40    NA    50 d     B     #> 5    50    25    10 e     A      # Non-numeric columns are ignored sum_n(df_mixed) #> [1] NA NA 65 NA 85  # Use inside mutate with mixed data df_mixed |> mutate(sum_score = sum_n(select = starts_with("var"))) #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>    var1  var2  var3 name  group sum_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr>     <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA a     A            NA #> 2    NA    NA    30 b     A            NA #> 3    30    15    20 c     B            65 #> 4    40    NA    50 d     B            NA #> 5    50    25    10 e     A            85  # Use everything(), but exclude known non-numeric sum_n(df_mixed, select = everything(), exclude = "group") #> [1] NA NA 65 NA 85  # Select columns using regex sum_n(df_mixed, select = "^var", regex = TRUE) #> [1] NA NA 65 NA 85 sum_n(df_mixed, select = "ar", regex = TRUE) #> [1] NA NA 65 NA 85  # Apply to a subset of rows df_mixed[1:3, ] |> sum_n(select = starts_with("var")) #> [1] NA NA 65  # Store the result in a new column df_mixed$sum_score <- sum_n(df_mixed, select = starts_with("var")) df_mixed #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>    var1  var2  var3 name  group sum_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr>     <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA a     A            NA #> 2    NA    NA    30 b     A            NA #> 3    30    15    20 c     B            65 #> 4    40    NA    50 d     B            NA #> 5    50    25    10 e     A            85  # With a numeric matrix mat <- matrix(c(1, 2, NA, 4, 5, NA, 7, 8, 9), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE) mat #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,]    1    2   NA #> [2,]    4    5   NA #> [3,]    7    8    9 mat |> sum_n(min_valid = 2) #> [1]  3  9 24

title: Categorical summary table — table_categorical
text: Builds publication-ready frequency cross-tabulation table one many categorical variables selected tidyselect syntax. , produces grouped cross-tabulation summaries (using cross_tab() internally) Chi-squared p-values optional association measures. Without , produces one-way frequency-style summaries. Multiple output formats available via output: printed ASCII table ("default"), wide long numeric data.frame ("data.frame", "long"), publication-ready tables ("tinytable", "gt", "flextable", "excel", "clipboard", "word").

title: Categorical summary table — table_categorical
code: table_categorical(   data,   select,   by = NULL,   labels = NULL,   levels_keep = NULL,   include_total = TRUE,   drop_na = TRUE,   weights = NULL,   rescale = FALSE,   correct = FALSE,   simulate_p = FALSE,   simulate_B = 2000,   percent_digits = 1,   p_digits = 3,   v_digits = 2,   assoc_measure = "auto",   assoc_ci = FALSE,   decimal_mark = ".",   align = c("decimal", "auto", "center", "right"),   output = c("default", "data.frame", "long", "tinytable", "gt", "flextable", "excel",     "clipboard", "word"),   indent_text = "  ",   indent_text_excel_clipboard = strrep(" ", 6),   add_multilevel_header = TRUE,   blank_na_wide = FALSE,   excel_path = NULL,   excel_sheet = "Categorical",   clipboard_delim = "\t",   word_path = NULL )

title: Categorical summary table — table_categorical
text: Depends output: "default": prints styled ASCII table returns underlying data.frame invisibly (S3 class "spicy_categorical_table"). "data.frame": wide data.frame one row per variable–level combination. used, columns Variable, Level, one pair n / \% columns per group level (plus Total include_total = TRUE), followed Chi2, df, p, association measure column. = NULL, columns Variable, Level, n, \%. "long": long data.frame columns variable, level, group, n, percent (chi2, df, p, association measure columns used). "tinytable": tinytable object. "gt": gt_tbl object. "flextable": flextable object. "excel" / "word": writes disk returns file path invisibly. "clipboard": copies table returns display data.frame invisibly.

title: Continuous summary table — table_continuous
text: Computes descriptive statistics (mean, SD, min, max, confidence interval mean, n) one many continuous variables selected tidyselect syntax. , produces grouped summaries reports group-comparison p-value default (Welch test; change via test). Additional inferential output opt-: test statistics (statistic) effect sizes (effect_size / effect_size_ci). Set p_value = FALSE suppress p-value column. Without , produces one-way descriptive summaries. Multiple output formats available via output: printed ASCII table ("default"), plain data.frame ("data.frame" "long" – synonyms underlying long-format data, see Details), publication-ready tables ("tinytable", "gt", "flextable", "excel", "clipboard", "word"). descriptive companion table_continuous_lm(). two functions share argument vocabulary (select, , weights / vcov exclusively model variant, effect_size, ci_level, digits, p_digits, decimal_mark, align, ...) descriptive analysis model-based analysis data use table layout, decimal mark, reporting precision.

title: Continuous summary table — table_continuous
code: table_continuous(   data,   select = tidyselect::everything(),   by = NULL,   exclude = NULL,   regex = FALSE,   test = c("welch", "student", "nonparametric"),   p_value = NULL,   statistic = FALSE,   show_n = TRUE,   effect_size = c("none", "auto", "hedges_g", "eta_sq", "r_rb", "epsilon_sq"),   effect_size_ci = FALSE,   ci = TRUE,   labels = NULL,   ci_level = 0.95,   digits = 2,   effect_size_digits = 2,   p_digits = 3,   decimal_mark = ".",   align = c("decimal", "auto", "center", "right"),   output = c("default", "data.frame", "long", "tinytable", "gt", "flextable", "excel",     "clipboard", "word"),   excel_path = NULL,   excel_sheet = "Descriptives",   clipboard_delim = "\t",   word_path = NULL,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Continuous summary table — table_continuous
text: Depends output: "default": prints styled ASCII table returns underlying data.frame invisibly (S3 class "spicy_continuous_table" / "spicy_table"). object can re-coerced via .data.frame.spicy_continuous_table() piped broom::tidy() / broom::glance(). "data.frame" / "long": plain data.frame columns variable, label, group (used), mean, sd, min, max, ci_lower, ci_upper, n. used together p_value = TRUE, statistic = TRUE, effect_size != "none", additional columns appended (populated first row variable block ): test_type – test identifier (e.g., "welch_t", "welch_anova", "student_t", "anova", "wilcoxon", "kruskal"). statistic, df1, df2, p.value – test results. es_type – effect-size identifier ("hedges_g", "eta_sq", "r_rb", "epsilon_sq"), effect_size != "none". es_value, es_ci_lower, es_ci_upper – effect-size estimate confidence interval bounds. two names "data.frame" "long" synonyms (descriptive output naturally already long). Pick whichever reads better code. "tinytable": tinytable object. "gt": gt_tbl object. "flextable": flextable object. "excel" / "word": writes disk returns file path invisibly. "clipboard": copies table returns display data.frame invisibly.

title: Continuous summary table — table_continuous
text: omnibus test computed supplied least two groups two observations. Choice driven test: "welch" (default): Welch t-test two groups (stats::t.test(var.equal = FALSE)); Welch one-way ANOVA three (stats::oneway.test(var.equal = FALSE)). assume equal variances. "student": Student t-test (var.equal = TRUE) / classical ANOVA (stats::oneway.test(var.equal = TRUE)). "nonparametric": Wilcoxon rank-sum / Mann-Whitney U two groups (stats::wilcox.test); Kruskal-Wallis H three (stats::kruskal.test). model-based contrasts (heteroskedasticity-consistent SE, cluster-robust SE, weighted contrasts, fitted means, etc.), use table_continuous_lm().

title: Continuous summary table — table_continuous
text: Effect size selected via effect_size. default "none" (column). "auto" mirrors historical effect_size = TRUE behaviour chooses canonical measure active (test, n_groups) combination: Parametric, 2 groups -> Hedges' g (Hedges & Olkin 1985). Parametric, 3+ groups -> Eta-squared (\(\eta^2\)). Nonparametric, 2 groups -> Rank-biserial r. Nonparametric, 3+ groups -> Epsilon-squared (\(\varepsilon^2\)). Explicit choices ("hedges_g", "eta_sq", "r_rb", "epsilon_sq") validated (test, n_groups); incompatible request triggers clear error rather silent fallback. model-based companion table_continuous_lm() adds Cohen's d, Hays' \(\omega^2\), Cohen's f\(^2\), derived fitted (possibly weighted) lm(). CIs available via effect_size_ci = TRUE: noncentral F inversion \(\eta^2\), Hedges-Olkin normal approximation g, Fisher z-transform r, percentile bootstrap (2 000 replicates) \(\varepsilon^2\).

title: Continuous summary table — table_continuous
text: default (align = "decimal") numeric columns aligned decimal mark, standard scientific-publication convention used SPSS, SAS, LaTeX siunitx, native primitives gt::cols_align_decimal() / tinytable::style_tt(align = "d"). engines without native primitive (flextable, word, clipboard, ASCII print), values pre-padded leading trailing spaces dots line vertically; flextable/word additionally use monospace font body. Pass align = "auto" revert legacy per-column rule (centre descriptive columns, right n p). p-values formatted p_digits decimal places (default 3, APA standard). threshold column shows <.001 10^{-p_digits}; setting p_digits = 4 shifts displayed precision threshold accordingly. Leading zeros p always stripped (.045, 0.045). Non-numeric columns silently dropped (set verbose = TRUE see columns excluded). single constant column passed, SD CI shown "--" ASCII table. Optional output engines require suggested packages: tinytable output = "tinytable" gt output = "gt" flextable output = "flextable" flextable + officer output = "word" openxlsx2 output = "excel" clipr output = "clipboard"

title: Continuous-outcome linear-model table — table_continuous_lm
text: Builds APA-style summary tables series simple linear models one many continuous outcomes selected tidyselect syntax. single predictor supplied , selected numeric outcome fit lm(outcome ~ , ...). categorical, function returns model-based mean-comparison table fitted means level derived linear model, plus optional single difference dichotomous predictors. numeric, table reports slope confidence interval. Multiple output formats available via output: printed ASCII table ("default"), plain wide data.frame ("data.frame"), raw long data.frame ("long"), rendered outputs ("tinytable", "gt", "flextable", "excel", "clipboard", "word").

title: Continuous-outcome linear-model table — table_continuous_lm
code: table_continuous_lm(   data,   select = tidyselect::everything(),   by,   exclude = NULL,   regex = FALSE,   weights = NULL,   vcov = c("classical", "HC0", "HC1", "HC2", "HC3", "HC4", "HC4m", "HC5", "CR0", "CR1",     "CR2", "CR3", "bootstrap", "jackknife"),   cluster = NULL,   boot_n = 1000,   contrast = c("auto", "none"),   statistic = FALSE,   p_value = TRUE,   show_n = TRUE,   show_weighted_n = FALSE,   effect_size = c("none", "f2", "d", "g", "omega2"),   effect_size_ci = FALSE,   r2 = c("r2", "adj_r2", "none"),   ci = TRUE,   labels = NULL,   ci_level = 0.95,   digits = 2,   fit_digits = 2,   effect_size_digits = 2,   p_digits = 3,   decimal_mark = ".",   align = c("decimal", "auto", "center", "right"),   output = c("default", "data.frame", "long", "tinytable", "gt", "flextable", "excel",     "clipboard", "word"),   excel_path = NULL,   excel_sheet = "Linear models",   clipboard_delim = "\t",   word_path = NULL,   verbose = FALSE )

One : "classical" (default): ordinary OLS/WLS variance vcov(lm), assumes homoscedastic errors. "HC0": original Eicker–White heteroskedasticity-consistent sandwich estimator (White 1980), finite-sample correction. "HC1": HC0 multiplied n / (n - p) (MacKinnon White 1985). Matches Stata's , robust default. "HC2": residuals divided sqrt(1 - h_ii) (MacKinnon White 1985). "HC3": residuals divided (1 - h_ii) (MacKinnon White 1985). common default small moderate samples (Long Ervin 2000). "HC4": leverage-adaptive variant designed influential observations (Cribari-Neto 2004). "HC4m": refinement HC4 modified leverage exponent (Cribari-Neto da Silva 2011). "HC5": alternative leverage-adaptive variant designed leveraged data (Cribari-Neto, Souza Vasconcellos 2007). "CR0", "CR1", "CR2", "CR3": cluster-robust sandwich estimators non-independent observations (Liang Zeger 1986); requires cluster. Inference asymptotic (z single contrasts, chi^2(q) global Wald test); CIs Wald-type around point estimate. "jackknife": leave-one-variance, leave-one-cluster-cluster supplied (Quenouille 1956; MacKinnon White 1985). Inference asymptotic (z / chi^2(q)). HC* variants computed via sandwich::vcovHC(). "long": raw data.frame one block per outcome 28 columns covering identification (variable, label, predictor_type, predictor_label, level, reference), fitted means CI (emmean, emmean_se, emmean_ci_lower, emmean_ci_upper), contrast slope estimates CI (estimate_type, estimate, estimate_se, estimate_ci_lower, estimate_ci_upper), inferential output (test_type, statistic, df1, df2, p.value), effect size CI (es_type, es_value, es_ci_lower, es_ci_upper), fit (r2, adj_r2), sample size (n, weighted_n). "tinytable": tinytable object. "gt": gt_tbl object. "flextable": flextable object. "excel" / "word": writes disk returns file path.
