text: table_continuous_lm() designed article-style bivariate reporting: single predictor supplied , one simple model per selected continuous outcome. model fit always lm(outcome ~ , ...), optionally weights. categorical predictors, reported means model-based fitted means level , contrasts derived fitted linear model. unweighted lm(y ~ factor) classical variance, fitted means coincide numerically empirical subgroup means; model-based qualifier matters () weights means become weighted least-squares estimates, (b) CIs derived model vcov (classical HC*), (c) tests, p-values, effect sizes come fitted model, keeping table internally consistent. Compared table_continuous(), function model-based companion: choose want heteroskedasticity-consistent standard errors (vcov = "HC*"), model fit statistics, case weights via lm(..., weights = ...). function exists report fitted model, inferential output default: p_value = TRUE r2 = "r2" defaults; set p_value = FALSE r2 = "none" suppress .

title: Continuous-outcome linear-model table — table_continuous_lm
text: Effect size selected explicitly via effect_size (defaults "none"). variants derived fitted model displayed coefficients, R², CIs, effect size stays internally consistent rest table. "f2": Cohen's f² = R² / (1 - R²) (Cohen 1988). Defined predictor type. single-predictor model, f² monotone transform R² adds information beyond ; primary use priori power analysis (e.g. G*Power). "d", "g": standardized mean difference (Cohen's d Hedges' g), defined exactly two non-empty levels. d = beta_hat / sigma_hat sigma_hat = summary(fit)$sigma (pooled within-group SD unweighted two-group case); g = J * d J = 1 - 3 / (4 * df_resid - 1) (Hedges Olkin 1985). sign matches displayed Delta (level2 - level1). published reports two-group comparisons, g convention recommended Hedges Olkin (1985). "omega2": Hays' \(\omega^2\), computed weighted sums squares (SS_effect - df_effect * MSE) / (SS_total + MSE) truncated 0 small null effects (Hays 1963; Olejnik Algina 2003). Less biased \(\eta^2\) (equals R^2 single-predictor design) recommended reporting variance explained ANOVA-style designs (Olejnik Algina 2003). four effect sizes point estimates derived OLS/WLS fit invariant vcov: choosing HC* changes SE, CI, test statistic contrast standardized magnitude . Confidence intervals effect size available via effect_size_ci = TRUE use modern noncentral-distribution inversion approach, consensus standard commercial statistical software (Stata esize / estat esize, SAS PROC TTEST PROC GLM EFFECTSIZE 14.2+) mainstream R packages (effectsize, MOTE, TOSTER, effsize): "d", "g": noncentral t inversion (Steiger Fouladi 1997; Goulet-Pelletier Cousineau 2018). Empirical coverage nominal across sample sizes (Cousineau Goulet-Pelletier 2021), unlike older Hedges-Olkin normal approximation biased small samples. Hedges' g bounds inherit J small-sample correction. "omega2", "f2": noncentral F inversion (Steiger 2004). Bounds converted noncentrality parameter using omega² = ncp / (ncp + N) f² = ncp / N respectively, N = df1 + df2 + 1 (total sample size). weighted case, CI uses raw (unweighted) group counts df.residual(fit) = n - p, consistent WLS reporting convention (DuMouchel Duncan 1983). propensity-score balance assessment complex-survey designs, dedicated packages (cobalt::bal.tab() Austin Stuart 2015 formulation; survey design-based effect sizes) appropriate.

title: Continuous-outcome linear-model table — table_continuous_lm
text: vcov one HC* variants, standard errors, CIs, Wald test statistics use heteroskedasticity-consistent sandwich estimator computed via sandwich::vcovHC() (Zeileis 2004), canonical R implementation. brief guide: "HC0" original White (1980) form; "HC1" adds n / (n - p) correction (MacKinnon White 1985), Stata's , robust default. "HC2" "HC3" use leverage-based residual rescalings (MacKinnon White 1985); "HC3" sandwich::vcovHC() default small moderate samples (Long Ervin 2000). "HC4" adapts leverage exponent influential observations (Cribari-Neto 2004); "HC4m" modified-exponent refinement (Cribari-Neto da Silva 2011); "HC5" alternative leverage-adaptive variant (Cribari-Neto, Souza Vasconcellos 2007). observations independent (repeated measurements per individual, students nested classes, patients hospitals, country-year panels), classical HC* standard errors biased downward. Use CR* variants together cluster = id_var get cluster-robust inference (Liang Zeger 1986). implementation dispatches clubSandwich::vcovCR() variance clubSandwich::coef_test() (single-coefficient, Satterthwaite t) clubSandwich::Wald_test() (multi-coefficient Hotelling-T-squared Satterthwaite df, "HTZ") inference. "CR2" (Bell McCaffrey 2002; Pustejovsky Tipton 2018) modern recommended default; generally produces fractional Satterthwaite degrees freedom df2, displayed t(df) / F(df1, df2) header renders one decimal. "CR1" matches Stata's , vce(cluster id). Effect sizes remain invariant vcov (including CR*); SE, CI, test statistic, df2 contrast change. Two resampling-based estimators also available without adding dependency: vcov = "bootstrap" (nonparametric resampling-cases bootstrap; Davison Hinkley 1997) vcov = "jackknife" (leave-one-delete-1; Quenouille 1956; MacKinnon White 1985). Supplying cluster switches cluster-aware variants (cluster bootstrap, Cameron, Gelbach Miller 2008; leave-one-cluster-jackknife). number bootstrap replicates controlled boot_n (default 1000); replicates fail fit rank-deficient resamples dropped, explicit warning half fail fallback classical OLS variance 10 valid replicates. Inference estimators asymptotic (z single-coefficient contrasts, chi^2(q) multi-coefficient global Wald test k > 2 categorical predictors), reflected displayed test header. Use bootstrap residual distribution non-standard sample small; use jackknife closed-form, deterministic alternative. R², adjusted R², effect sizes remain ordinary least-squares (weighted least-squares) statistics regardless vcov.

title: Continuous-outcome linear-model table — table_continuous_lm
code: # --- Basic usage ---------------------------------------------------------  # Default: ASCII table with model-based means, p, and R². table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67.16      71.05          3.89         #>  Body mass index               │   25.69      26.20          0.51         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.13       5.64     <.001  0.02  1200  #>  Body mass index               │   0.09       0.93      .018  0.00  1188   # --- Effect sizes -------------------------------------------------------  # Cohen's d (binary by required). table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   effect_size = "d" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67.16      71.05          3.89         #>  Body mass index               │   25.69      26.20          0.51         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    d     n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.13       5.64     <.001  0.02  0.25  1200  #>  Body mass index               │   0.09       0.93      .018  0.00  0.14  1188   # Hedges' g with weighted analysis and weighted n column. table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   weights = weight,   statistic = TRUE,   effect_size = "g",   show_weighted_n = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67.01      70.88          3.87         #>  Body mass index               │   25.51      25.98          0.47         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL   t      p     R²    g    #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.11       5.62     4.33  <.001  0.02  0.25  #>  Body mass index               │   0.05       0.89     2.18   .030  0.00  0.13  #>  #>  Variable                      │  n    Weighted n  #> ───────────────────────────────┼────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 1200   1196.47    #>  Body mass index               │ 1188   1183.32     # Hedges' g with noncentral t confidence interval (bracket notation). table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   effect_size = "g",   effect_size_ci = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67.16      71.05          3.89         #>  Body mass index               │   25.69      26.20          0.51         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²   #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.13       5.64     <.001  0.02  #>  Body mass index               │   0.09       0.93      .018  0.00  #>  #>  Variable                      │         g           n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 0.25 [0.14, 0.36]  1200  #>  Body mass index               │ 0.14 [0.02, 0.25]  1188   # Cohen's f² alongside R² (familiar power-analysis effect size). table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   effect_size = "f2" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67.16      71.05          3.89         #>  Body mass index               │   25.69      26.20          0.51         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    f²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.13       5.64     <.001  0.02  0.02  1200  #>  Body mass index               │   0.09       0.93      .018  0.00  0.00  1188   # Hays' omega-squared for a 3-level predictor (d / g would error here). table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = education,   effect_size = "omega2" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Highest education level #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Lower secondary)  M (Upper secondary)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │        67.68                81.56         #>  Body mass index               │        26.17                23.55         #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Tertiary)    p     R²    ω²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │    66.10      <.001  0.21  0.21  1200  #>  Body mass index               │    26.35      <.001  0.13  0.13  1188   # --- Robust SE for a numeric predictor ----------------------------------  # HC3 standard errors for the slope of a continuous predictor. table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = age,   vcov = "HC3",   ci = FALSE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Age (years) #>  #>  Variable                        │   B        p       R²      n     #> ─────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)   │  0.04     .176    0.00    1200   #>  Body mass index                 │  0.04    <.001    0.02    1188    # Cluster-robust SE for repeated-measures data: the `sleep` dataset # has 10 subjects measured twice (one observation per group). table_continuous_lm(   sleep,   select = extra,   by = group,   cluster = ID,   vcov = "CR2" ) #> Registered S3 method overwritten by 'clubSandwich': #>   method    from     #>   bread.mlm sandwich #> Continuous outcomes by group #>  #>  Variable │ M (1)  M (2)  Δ (2 - 1)  95% CI LL  95% CI UL   p     R²   n   #> ──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  extra    │ 0.75   2.33     1.58       0.70       2.46     .003  0.16  20   # --- Article-style polish -----------------------------------------------  # Pretty outcome labels and adjusted R². table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   labels = c(     wellbeing_score = "WHO-5 wellbeing (0-100)",     bmi = "Body-mass index (kg/m²)"   ),   r2 = "adj_r2" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  95% CI LL  #> ─────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing (0-100) │   67.16      71.05          3.89           2.13     #>  Body-mass index (kg/m²) │   25.69      26.20          0.51           0.09     #>  #>  Variable                │ 95% CI UL    p    Adj. R²   n    #> ─────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing (0-100) │   5.64     <.001   0.01    1200  #>  Body-mass index (kg/m²) │   0.93      .018   0.00    1188   # European decimal comma. table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   decimal_mark = "," ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67,16      71,05          3,89         #>  Body mass index               │   25,69      26,20          0,51         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2,13       5,64     <,001  0,02  1200  #>  Body mass index               │   0,09       0,93      ,018  0,00  1188   # Regex selection of all columns starting with "life_sat". table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = "^life_sat",   by = sex,   regex = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                                   │ M (Female)  M (Male)  #> ────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────── #>  Satisfaction with health (1-5)             │    3.51       3.59    #>  Satisfaction with work (1-5)               │    3.32       3.44    #>  Satisfaction with relationships (1-5)      │    3.71       3.74    #>  Satisfaction with standard of living (1-5) │    3.37       3.42    #>  #>  Variable                                   │ Δ (Male - Female)  95% CI LL  #> ────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────── #>  Satisfaction with health (1-5)             │       0.08           -0.06    #>  Satisfaction with work (1-5)               │       0.12           -0.01    #>  Satisfaction with relationships (1-5)      │       0.04           -0.09    #>  Satisfaction with standard of living (1-5) │       0.05           -0.08    #>  #>  Variable                                   │ 95% CI UL   p     R²    n    #> ────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────── #>  Satisfaction with health (1-5)             │   0.22     .267  0.00  1192  #>  Satisfaction with work (1-5)               │   0.26     .073  0.00  1192  #>  Satisfaction with relationships (1-5)      │   0.16     .570  0.00  1192  #>  Satisfaction with standard of living (1-5) │   0.18     .453  0.00  1192   # --- Output formats -----------------------------------------------------  # The rendered outputs below all wrap the same call: #   table_continuous_lm(sochealth, #                       select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi), #                       by = sex) # only `output` changes. Assign to a variable to avoid the # console-friendly text fallback that some engines fall back to # when printed directly in `?` help.  # Wide data.frame (one row per outcome). table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   output = "data.frame" ) #>                                      Variable M (Female) M (Male) #> wellbeing_score WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)   67.16194 71.04879 #> bmi                           Body mass index   25.68506 26.19685 #>                 Δ (Male - Female)  95% CI LL 95% CI UL            p          R² #> wellbeing_score         3.8868576 2.12952733 5.6441879 1.548898e-05 0.015475137 #> bmi                     0.5117882 0.08879857 0.9347778 1.776254e-02 0.004728908 #>                    n #> wellbeing_score 1200 #> bmi             1188  # Raw long data.frame (one block per outcome). table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   output = "long" ) #>          variable                         label predictor_type predictor_label #> 1 wellbeing_score WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)    categorical             Sex #> 2 wellbeing_score WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)    categorical             Sex #> 3             bmi               Body mass index    categorical             Sex #> 4             bmi               Body mass index    categorical             Sex #>    level reference estimate_type   emmean emmean_se emmean_ci_lower #> 1 Female    Female          <NA> 67.16194 0.6227155        65.94020 #> 2   Male    Female    difference 71.04879 0.6438305        69.78563 #> 3 Female    Female          <NA> 25.68506 0.1495988        25.39156 #> 4   Male    Female    difference 26.19685 0.1552460        25.89227 #>   emmean_ci_upper  estimate estimate_se estimate_ci_lower estimate_ci_upper #> 1        68.38367        NA          NA                NA                NA #> 2        72.31195 3.8868576   0.8957077        2.12952733         5.6441879 #> 3        25.97857        NA          NA                NA                NA #> 4        26.50144 0.5117882   0.2155948        0.08879857         0.9347778 #>   test_type statistic df1  df2      p.value es_type es_value es_ci_lower #> 1         F 18.830621   1 1198 1.548898e-05    <NA>       NA          NA #> 2         t  4.339426   1 1198 1.548898e-05    <NA>       NA          NA #> 3         F  5.635133   1 1186 1.776254e-02    <NA>       NA          NA #> 4         t  2.373843   1 1186 1.776254e-02    <NA>       NA          NA #>   es_ci_upper          r2      adj_r2    n weighted_n #> 1          NA 0.015475137 0.014653330 1200         NA #> 2          NA          NA          NA 1200         NA #> 3          NA 0.004728908 0.003889725 1188         NA #> 4          NA          NA          NA 1188         NA  # \donttest{ # Rendered HTML / docx objects -- best viewed inside a # Quarto / R Markdown document or a pkgdown article. if (requireNamespace("tinytable", quietly = TRUE)) {   tt <- table_continuous_lm(     sochealth, select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi), by = sex,     output = "tinytable"   ) } if (requireNamespace("gt", quietly = TRUE)) {   tbl <- table_continuous_lm(     sochealth, select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi), by = sex,     output = "gt"   ) } if (requireNamespace("flextable", quietly = TRUE)) {   ft <- table_continuous_lm(     sochealth, select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi), by = sex,     output = "flextable"   ) }  # Excel and Word: write to a temporary file. if (requireNamespace("openxlsx2", quietly = TRUE)) {   tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".xlsx")   table_continuous_lm(     sochealth, select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi), by = sex,     output = "excel", excel_path = tmp   )   unlink(tmp) } if (   requireNamespace("flextable", quietly = TRUE) &&     requireNamespace("officer", quietly = TRUE) ) {   tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".docx")   table_continuous_lm(     sochealth, select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi), by = sex,     output = "word", word_path = tmp   )   unlink(tmp) } # }  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Clipboard: writes to the system clipboard. table_continuous_lm(   sochealth, select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi), by = sex,   output = "clipboard" ) } # }

title: Uncertainty Coefficient — uncertainty_coef
text: uncertainty_coef() computes Uncertainty Coefficient (Theil's U) two-way contingency table, based information entropy.

title: Uncertainty Coefficient — uncertainty_coef
code: uncertainty_coef(   x,   direction = c("symmetric", "row", "column"),   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   digits = 3L,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Uncertainty Coefficient — uncertainty_coef
text: x contingency table (class table). direction Direction prediction: "symmetric" (default), "row" (column predicts row), "column" (row predicts column). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Uncertainty Coefficient — uncertainty_coef
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: U = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Uncertainty Coefficient — uncertainty_coef
text: uncertainty coefficient measures association using Shannon entropy. direction = "row": \(U = (H_X + H_Y - H_{XY}) / H_X\), \(H_X\), \(H_Y\) marginal entropies \(H_{XY}\) joint entropy. symmetric version \(U = 2 (H_X + H_Y - H_{XY}) / (H_X + H_Y)\). entropy terms use standard mathematical convention \(0 \log 0 = 0\), matching SPSS / PSPP CROSSTABS definition Cover & Thomas (2006). Note DescTools::UncertCoef() applies additional Laplace correction (replacing zero cells \(1/n^2\)) entropy computation, produces slightly different point estimates tables empty cells; correction uncommon information-theory literature used . asymptotic standard errors follow DescTools delta method; see cramer_v() full references.

title: Uncertainty Coefficient — uncertainty_coef
code: tab <- table(sochealth$smoking, sochealth$education) uncertainty_coef(tab) #> [1] 0.01148762 uncertainty_coef(tab, direction = "row", detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.018     0.003     0.032  0.021

title: Generate a comprehensive summary of the variables — varlist
code: varlist(   x,   ...,   values = FALSE,   tbl = FALSE,   include_na = FALSE,   factor_levels = c("observed", "all") )  vl(   x,   ...,   values = FALSE,   tbl = FALSE,   include_na = FALSE,   factor_levels = c("observed", "all") )

title: Generate a comprehensive summary of the variables — varlist
text: x data frame, transformation one. ... Optional tidyselect-style column selectors (e.g. starts_with("var"), (.numeric), etc.). Columns can selected reordered, renaming selections supported. values Logical. FALSE (default), displays compact summary variable's values. numeric, character, date/time, labelled, factor variables, unique non-missing values shown four; otherwise first three values, ellipsis (...), last value shown. Values sorted appropriate (e.g., numeric, character, date). factors, factor_levels controls whether observed declared levels shown; level order preserved. labelled variables, prefixed labels displayed via labelled::to_factor(levels = "prefixed"). TRUE, unique non-missing values displayed. tbl Logical. FALSE (default), opens summary Viewer session interactive. TRUE, returns tibble. include_na Logical. TRUE, unique missing value markers (<NA>, <NaN>) explicitly appended end Values summary present variable. applies variable types. Literal strings "NA", "NaN", "" quoted distinguish missing markers. FALSE (default), missing values omitted Values still counted NAs column. factor_levels Character. Controls factor values displayed Values. "observed" (default; code_book() uses "") shows levels present data, preserving factor level order. "" shows declared levels, including unused levels.

title: Generate a comprehensive summary of the variables — varlist
text: tibble one row per selected variable, containing following columns: Variable: variable names Label: variable labels (available via label attribute) Values: summary variable's values, depending values include_na arguments. values = FALSE, compact summary shown: unique values four, otherwise 3 + ... + last. values = TRUE, unique non-missing values displayed. labelled variables, prefixed labels displayed using labelled::to_factor(levels = "prefixed"). factors, levels displayed according factor_levels. Matrix array columns summarized dimensions. Missing value markers (<NA>, <NaN>) optionally appended end (controlled via include_na). Literal strings "NA", "NaN", "" quoted distinguish missing markers. title: Yule's Q — yule_q

title: Yule's Q — yule_q
code: yule_q(x, detail = FALSE, conf_level = 0.95, digits = 3L, .include_se = FALSE)

title: Yule's Q — yule_q
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Yule's Q — yule_q
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: Q = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Yule's Q — yule_q
text: 2x2 table cells \(, b, c, d\), Yule's Q \(Q = (ad - bc) / (ad + bc)\). equivalent Goodman-Kruskal Gamma 2x2 tables. asymptotic standard error \(SE = 0.5 (1 - Q^2) \sqrt{1/+ 1/b + 1/c + 1/d}\). Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: spicy 0.1.0
text: Introduces collection tools variable inspection, descriptive summaries, data exploration. Extract variable metadata display compact summaries (varlist()). Compute frequency tables (freq()), cross-tabulations (cross_tab()), Cramer’s V categorical associations (cramer_v()). Generate descriptive statistics means (mean_n()), sums (sum_n()), counts (count_n()) automatic handling missing data. Copy data (copy_clipboard()) directly clipboard quick export.
